title: Move DIV/REM Off The ALU Critical Path
motivation:
The current EX-stage ALU is fully combinational and includes RV32M divide
and remainder operators in its worst-case path. On the Tang Nano 20K that
logic is likely much slower than the add/shift/branch hot path, yet
CoreMark's timed region is dominated by list/state/matrix work and appears
to use division only in cold setup code. If the divider stops limiting Fmax,
a small stall cost on rare DIV/REM instructions should buy a net fitness
gain.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the single-cycle combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU logic with a small
iterative divider in EX, while keeping ADD/logic/shift/branch and the
multiply family on the existing fast path. EX would assert a busy condition
only for divide/remainder ops, hold younger stages until the result is ready,
then release a single normal retirement with the same RV32M corner-case and
RVFI semantics. This should shorten the worst timing path, trim LUT pressure,
and preserve CoreMark/MHz because the hot benchmark loop is not divider
heavy.

Add an iterative divider path in EX and hold EX/MEM advance until DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU completes.

Add coverage for multi-cycle DIV/REM stalls, forwarding, and single-channel RVFI retirement behavior.

title: Stall-Only Fetch Replay Buffer
motivation:
The bench core turns every random `io_imemReady=0` cycle into a frontend
bubble even when the same hot-loop PC has already been fetched many times.
Under the tournament's fixed ~22% instruction-stall model, recovering even a
fraction of those lost fetches should raise iterations/cycle without
widening retirement or touching the already timing-sensitive EX datapath.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny direct-mapped instruction replay buffer in IF that records
`{pc, instr}` on successful live fetches and is consulted only when the
external imem port is not ready. On an imem-stall hit, IF should supply the
cached instruction and assert an effective fetch-ready signal so decode
advances instead of seeing a forced bubble. Keep the zero-wait path direct
when `io_imemReady=1`, and preserve the current redirect, reset, and RVFI
retirement behavior. This targets the benchmark's synthetic iStall bubbles
directly while avoiding new pressure on the ALU, load-use path, or dmem
timing.

Expose an IF-stage fetch-available signal to the top level and route it into hazard control so a replay hit behaves like a ready fetch instead of a forced bubble.
Base frontend stalling and flushing on effective fetch availability rather than raw `io_imemReady`, so only true imem misses inject bubbles while replay hits let IF/ID and ID/EX continue.

title: Static Backward-Branch IF Predictor
motivation:
The current bench core resolves every conditional branch in EX and always
fetches `pc+4` until that point, so taken loop branches pay a two-bubble
wrong-path recovery cost. CoreMark spends much of its timed region in short
backward loops, making a tiny static branch predictor a strong IPC lever
without the LUT and routing cost of a learned table, BTB, or return stack.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a decode-light IF-stage predictor that recognizes legal B-type branches
and predicts backward branches taken when the branch immediate is negative.
IF computes the B immediate and predicted target, carries
`predicted_taken/predicted_target` through the existing pipeline metadata,
and EX redirects only when the resolved branch next PC disagrees with that
prediction. JAL and JALR stay on the current EX redirect path so the change
stays focused on the hottest control-flow class while keeping timing and LUT
growth small.

title: Move Cold MULH Ops Off The EX Hot Path
motivation:
The current bench ALU still computes three separate sign/zero-extended
multiply products in the one-cycle EX datapath, and each product is written
as a 64-bit-by-64-bit expression even though the ISA operands are only 32
bits wide. That is a strong candidate for the remaining Fmax limiter after
DIV/REM was moved out of the hot path. CoreMark's timed region is multiply
heavy because of the matrix kernel, but it overwhelmingly cares about plain
low-half `MUL`; the high-half multiply variants are correctness-critical yet
benchmark-cold.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep plain `MUL` as the hot single-cycle multiply, but remove `MULH`,
`MULHU`, and `MULHSU` from the always-hot combinational ALU path. In
`rtl/alu.sv`, collapse the current triple-product implementation so the fast
path only needs one correctly-sized low-half multiply for `MUL` plus the
existing simple ALU ops. In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, add a small cold helper for
the high-half multiply variants, reusing the existing long-op stall pattern
used by DIV/REM so those rare instructions remain architecturally correct
without capping global Fmax. This should preserve CoreMark IPC much better
than making all multiply ops iterative, while still buying timing slack by
deleting the widest multiply logic from the common EX result cone.

Add pipeline-level checks that high-half multiply variants stall only while their helper runs, retire exactly once on channel 0, and still forward the completed result correctly.

title: One-Deep Stalled-Store Retirement Slot
motivation:
The bench core already got its biggest measured win by shortening the EX hot
path with the DIV/REM side unit, and the two failed bench structural attempts
both showed that adding more logic to IF is a bad trade on this snapshot.
The cleaner remaining structural loss is on the data side: every
`io_dmemReady=0` cycle for a STORE still freezes the whole pipeline even
though the store has no register result and its address, byte mask, and write
data are already known in MEM.

both showed that adding more logic to IF is a bad trade on this snapshot.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-deep pending-store retirement slot that is used only for legal,
aligned normal-RAM stores when they reach MEM and the external dmem port is
stalled. Keep the existing zero-wait store fast path unchanged. On a stalled
RAM store, capture the store's byte address, replicated write data, byte
mask, and RVFI-visible memory fields into the slot, retire that store once on
channel 0, and let younger non-memory instructions continue while the slot
drains oldest-first onto dmem. Loads, later memory operations, and all
MMIO/UART/marker accesses must still wait behind the slot so ordering,
CoreMark timing markers, and RVFI semantics stay intact. This targets the
tournament's random dStall bubbles without reopening the frontend critical
path or putting load data onto the EX operand muxes.

Refine the dmem-stall policy so a store that has already retired into the pending slot no longer freezes unrelated younger ALU/control instructions, while still blocking younger memory operations and any case that would violate in-order behavior.

title: Tiny PC-Indexed Taken BTB
motivation:
The last predictor attempt lost fitness because it lengthened the frontend
critical path with `imem_data` decode and branch-immediate construction in IF.
But the branch penalty is still real: this core waits until EX to correct any
taken control flow, so hot loops and repeated calls keep paying wrong-path
fetch bubbles. The most promising predictor angle is therefore one that keeps
prediction state PC-indexed and decode-free on the fetch path.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a very small direct-mapped branch target buffer that is consulted by PC
alone and predicts only previously-taken control-flow sites. IF should use a
`valid/tag/target` BTB hit to fetch the remembered target instead of `pc+4`,
without decoding the fetched instruction or rebuilding immediates on the hot
path. EX remains the point of truth for branch direction, JAL/JALR targets,
traps, and RVFI; it simply compares the resolved next PC against the carried
prediction metadata, redirects on misses, and updates or invalidates the BTB
entry. This should recover much of the loop/call bubble cost while avoiding
the timing regression seen from instruction-driven IF prediction logic.

title: Share One RV32M Multiplier Datapath
motivation:
The current bench ALU still pays for three parallel multiply datapaths on
every EX cycle: signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, and signed*unsigned. The
last MULH offload attempt showed that adding new long-op control can erase
the timing win, but the existing `alu.sv` structure is still a strong Fmax
risk because it duplicates wide multiply hardware even though CoreMark's hot
loop mostly needs plain low-half `MUL`.

last MULH offload attempt showed that adding new long-op control can erase

risk because it duplicates wide multiply hardware even though CoreMark's hot
loop mostly needs plain low-half `MUL`.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Rework `rtl/alu.sv` so all four multiply-family ops stay single-cycle, but
are implemented from one correctly-sized shared 32x32 product datapath plus
small sign-correction logic for the high-half variants instead of three
separate products. This keeps the divider/store-slot wins intact, avoids new
EX stall or result-retime machinery, and attacks the same remaining RV32M
timing cost that motivated the failed MULH split with a much lighter control
footprint. If Yosys/nextpnr currently map the three products into duplicated
DSP or LUT fabric, collapsing them into one multiplier should improve Fmax
and area with essentially no CoreMark IPC cost.

title: Two-Deep RAM Store Retirement Queue
motivation:
The first MEM-side store slot already proved that this snapshot gains more
from data-side decoupling than from extra frontend logic. The remaining
structural bottleneck is that the current one-entry pending-store design
still serializes every second normal-RAM store behind the first pending
one, even though both stores already have fully computed address, mask, and
write data and neither produces a register result.

one, even though both stores already have fully computed address, mask, and
write data and neither produces a register result.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the one-entry pending-store slot in `mem_stage.sv` with a tiny
two-entry oldest-first queue for legal, aligned normal-RAM stores. Keep the
zero-wait store fast path unchanged, retire a store once when it is
enqueued, and let younger non-memory instructions continue while one or two
queued stores drain onto dmem. Loads, MMIO/marker accesses, and any later
memory op must still wait behind the queue for ordering and RVFI
correctness. This extends the only structural direction that has already
improved fitness, while staying off the timing-sensitive IF and EX paths.

title: One-Entry Loop Target Predictor
motivation:
The bench core still pays a full control-flow bubble whenever a hot loop
backedge is taken, but the two prior predictor attempts showed that this
design cannot afford much extra logic on the fetch critical path. The next
predictor should therefore recover only the highest-value target predictions
while keeping IF hardware smaller than both an instruction-driven predictor
and a multi-entry BTB.

hypothesis.motivation:

design cannot afford much extra logic on the fetch critical path. The next
predictor should therefore recover only the highest-value target predictions
while keeping IF hardware smaller than both an instruction-driven predictor
and a multi-entry BTB.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry loop-target predictor that remembers only the most recent
taken backward branch and predicts again only on an exact PC match. IF would
consult one registered `{valid, branch_pc, target_pc}` entry and choose the
remembered target instead of `pc+4` on a hit, with no `imem_data` decode,
immediate construction, or table RAM lookup on the hot path. EX remains the
point of truth: it validates the carried prediction, redirects only on
mispredicts, and updates or invalidates the single entry when a backward
branch is taken or falls through. This keeps the predictor focused on
CoreMark's dominant tight loops while making the IF cost much smaller than
the previously-regressing BTB approach.

title: DSP-Friendly RV32M Multiplier Sizing
motivation:
The current bench ALU still encodes its multiply family as three always-live
64-bit-extended products, even though RV32M only needs 32x32 operand widths.
The last multiplier experiments showed that adding new long-op control or
sign-correction chains is a poor trade here, so the cleaner remaining angle
is to keep the existing single-cycle behavior but make the multiplier coding
friendlier to Gowin DSP inference and shorter routing.

correctly sized 32x32 or 33x33 signed/unsigned products instead of the
current sign/zero-extended 64-bit operand expressions. Preserve the present
one-cycle RV32M contract, the ALTOPS branch, and the existing simple-ALU
fast path; the change is purely about expressing the multiply datapaths in a
form that lets Yosys/nextpnr infer smaller, faster hardware without adding
any new EX stalls or high-half post-processing. If the current coding style
is blocking clean DSP mapping, this should improve Fmax with essentially no
CoreMark IPC penalty.

title: One-Deep Ordered Long-Op Shadow Slot
motivation:
Frontend-side structural ideas have already regressed on this core, while the
one-entry RAM-store slot improved fitness by exploiting the benchmark's
synthetic dmem stalls without touching IF. The remaining structural waste is
that any stalled LOAD or in-flight DIV/REM still freezes the whole machine
even when the next instruction is an independent short ALU op whose result
could be computed and parked cheaply.

title: Direction-Only Backward-Branch Hold Predictor
motivation:
Every prior bench predictor lost fitness because it inserted compare, target,
or decode logic directly into the IF `pc -> next_pc` path. But this core
still wastes two sequential fetch slots on taken branches before EX resolves
them. A predictor that reduces that waste without changing IF target
selection is the cleanest remaining control-flow lever.

selection is the cleanest remaining control-flow lever.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Reuse the existing EX redirect point, but add a very cheap static direction
predictor that treats legal backward conditional branches as likely taken.
When such a branch reaches EX, `hazard_unit` should hold the frontend for one
cycle and inject a bubble into ID instead of allowing a second wrong-path
sequential fetch. If the branch is actually taken, EX redirects exactly as it
does today and the branch penalty drops from two wrong-path fetches to one.

loop exit. This stays off the timing-sensitive IF target path because it adds
no BTB lookup, no tag compare, and no target mux on `pc+4` selection.

title: Decode-Aware Load-Use Stall Filter
motivation:
The current bench core has already shown that small, timing-friendly changes
can beat larger frontend ideas, but its load-use hazard check is still more
conservative than the ISA requires. `hazard_unit.sv` stalls whenever a load

younger opcode does not actually read that source. In a load-heavy CoreMark
mix, that creates avoidable bubbles after immediates, jumps, LUI/AUIPC, and
other single-source instructions whose unused bitfields alias the older
load's `rd`.

motivation:
The bench core's biggest proven gains have come from shortening hot timing
paths, and the repeated predictor/replay regressions point at the frontend
as especially fragile when extra logic lands on `pc -> next_pc`. This core
still has a long fetch-to-decode path because `if_stage` emits a
combinational IF/ID payload that immediately drives decode, immediate
generation, regfile addressing, and the ID/EX register in the same cycle.
A pure stage retime attacks that path directly without adding new prediction
logic to IF.

title: Latched-Instruction Load-Use Stall Filter
motivation:
The strongest remaining low-risk IPC loss in `cores/bench` is the overly
conservative load-use detector. After a LOAD in EX, `hazard_unit.sv`
currently stalls on any raw IF/ID `rs1` or `rs2` bitfield match, even when
the younger opcode does not architecturally read that field. CoreMark's mix
includes many I-type ALU ops, loads, JAL/JALR, LUI/AUIPC, FENCE, and illegal
or SYSTEM encodings, so those false matches can create avoidable one-cycle
bubbles without buying correctness.

IF/ID instruction into `hazard_unit.sv`, derive compact `uses_rs1` and

bubble when the younger instruction truly consumes the matching source
register. Do not modify decode-stage source addresses or values, and do not
touch RVFI source reporting. This stays off the fetch `pc -> next_pc`
critical path, preserves the existing redirect and memory-stall precedence,
and should finally realize the IPC gain that the earlier broken load-use
attempts were targeting without repeating their contract bug.

title: Covered Same-Word Store-Slot Load Forwarding
motivation:
The current bench core has already shown that MEM-side decoupling pays, but
the pending-store slot is still conservative for same-word aliases. When an
older buffered RAM store already determines every byte a younger load wants,
the core still stalls until the physical store drains, throwing away cycles
under the benchmark's random dmem backpressure.

normal-RAM load to the same word can complete directly from slot data when
the slot's byte mask fully covers the load's requested bytes. Keep the
current different-word bypass, keep MMIO and marker accesses unbuffered, and
keep partial-overlap cases conservative by waiting behind the slot unless
every loaded byte is known from the buffered store. This stays off the
timing-sensitive frontend, adds no extra retirement slot or queue depth, and
turns more dStall windows into useful forward progress with strictly ordered
RVFI-visible memory behavior.

title: Learned EX-Stage Loop Hold Predictor
motivation:
Every predictor that steered fetch earlier regressed because this core's
frontend is extremely sensitive to extra `pc -> next_pc` logic, yet the
branch penalty is still real. The least-explored remaining predictor angle
is to leave target selection exactly where it is today and predict only
whether a branch is worth pre-emptively holding for, so hot taken loop
backedges lose less fetch bandwidth without paying a bubble on every static
backedge.

selection, remember the most recent taken backward branch PC (or a similarly
tiny exact-PC taken history), and use that state to request the existing
one-cycle frontend hold only for branches predicted taken. If the branch is
actually taken, the core keeps the current EX redirect path but avoids the
second wrong-path sequential fetch; if the branch falls through, the cost is
only one bubble on a real misprediction rather than on every loop-closing
branch. This stays off the fragile fetch critical path, removes the static
hold predictor's guaranteed-loop-exit penalty, and targets CoreMark's hot
repeating loop backedges with much less control-path cost than IF- or
ID-stage redirection.

motivation:
The recent load-use-filter attempts suggest the bottleneck is real, but
qualifying source usage in `hazard_unit.sv` adds logic on the fragile
IF/ID control path and already regressed. The current core still pays a
guaranteed one-cycle bubble after every dependent load even when the older
load reaches MEM on a cycle where `io_dmemReady=1` and the data is already
available.

title: Pending-Slot Same-Word Store Coalescing
motivation:
Frontend structural ideas have consistently hurt this core, while the only
repeatable wins have come from keeping work off IF and exploiting the
existing one-entry RAM store slot. After the recent different-word load
bypass improvement, the remaining conservative data-side stall is clustered
normal-RAM stores to the same aligned word: once the slot is full, every
later store waits even though its effect could be folded into the already
buffered word without touching the timing-sensitive load or fetch paths.

cost.

function calls, but a return target can come from a tiny RAS instead of

title: Trim EX Redirect and DIV Snapshot
motivation:
The current bench core already won by moving DIV/REM off the always-hot ALU
path, but EX still carries extra routing and state that no longer buys IPC.
`ex_stage.sv` snapshots a full `id_ex_t` across divides even though only a
small subset is consumed on completion, and `ex_mem_t` still includes branch
metadata that never reaches later logic or RVFI. On a small FPGA, that kind
of dead payload and duplicate control-path arithmetic can cost Fmax even when
architectural behavior is unchanged.

motivation:

title: Reset-Light Write-First Register File
motivation:
The bench core still carries a synchronous clear across the entire 32x32
integer register file, and the current unit test suite even enforces that
non-architectural reset-all-zero behavior. On a small FPGA, that means more
than a thousand datapath bits sit on the reset network and may block a
denser LUTRAM-style implementation or at least add reset mux/routing cost.
CoreMark's startup code explicitly initializes the live-in software state
(`sp`, `a0`, `a1`), so paying that reset cost on every GPR is a poor fit
for a fitness metric that is purely Fmax times steady-state IPC.

pre-existing dead cycles instead of paying extra ready-cycle timing cost.

motivation:
The structural ideas that added logic to IF or made the pending-store slot

title: Reset-Light Divider And Store Helper State
motivation:
The strongest bench gain so far came from removing unnecessary reset load from
the always-hot register file, but the later whole-pipeline reset-light attempt
regressed because it touched hot interstage state. The remaining reset-heavy
logic now sits mostly in cold helper blocks: the iterative divider datapath,
the EX-stage divide snapshot payload, and the pending-store slot in MEM.
Those words are only consumed when separate valid or state bits are asserted,
so forcing them onto reset is likely pure routing and mux cost.

Keep the pipeline registers and frontend untouched, but make only the cold
helper payloads resetless. In `rtl/div_unit.sv`, reset only the FSM state and
overwrite datapath registers on `start`; in `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, reset only
`div_active_q` and `div_result_valid_q` while treating the saved divide
payload as don't-care until a launch; in `rtl/mem_stage.sv`, reset only
`slot_valid_q` and let slot address, data, and mask be overwritten on
enqueue. This should preserve IPC and architectural behavior because the
existing valid or state bits already gate every use of that data, while
cutting reset fanout and reset-data muxing in logic that sits near the
successful divider and store-slot optimizations without reopening the fragile
IF or interstage timing paths.

title: Predecoded MEM Request Descriptor Pipe
motivation:
Structural ideas that touched IF or made the pending-store slot smarter have
mostly regressed, but the current `mem_stage.sv` is now a dense always-hot
block after the successful store-slot and different-word-load-bypass work.
Every cycle it recomputes byte masks, replicated store data, aligned
addresses, misalignment checks, and slot-arbitration inputs from the raw EX
payload. Repartitioning that work is a cleaner structural target than adding
more speculation or a more complex store buffer.

title: Registered One-Cycle Exact JAL Predictor
motivation:
Predictor work on this core has repeatedly failed when it adds same-cycle
decode or compare logic to the fragile `pc -> next_pc` path, but the exact
`JAL` redirect was the closest predictor miss so far. That suggests the bad
trade is the direct ID-to-PC feedback loop, not the idea of recovering one
unconditional-jump bubble itself.

hypothesis.motivation:

unconditional-jump bubble itself.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny deferred exact-redirect predictor for `JAL` only. When ID decodes
a legal `JAL`, latch a one-entry `{valid, pc, target}` record and let IF
consume that registered target on the following cycle instead of `pc+4`, so
the fetch-side decision is driven only by flop outputs rather than a live
decode/immediate/adder feedback path. Carry exact-prediction metadata into EX
so `JAL` still behaves as a normal architectural control transfer there:
EX should keep the canonical `pc_next`, `rd_wdata = pc+4`, and RVFI behavior,
and suppress only the redundant physical redirect when the registered target
already won. This should recover one wrong-path fetch on every `JAL` or call
with lower Fmax risk than the prior ID-stage exact redirect, while avoiding
the rare-activation limit and architectural desynchronization bug of the
stall-window variant.

title: Opcode-Aware Load-Use Stall Filter
motivation:
`hazard_unit.sv` currently stalls after a load whenever the older `rd`
matches either raw IF/ID source field, even when the younger instruction
does not architecturally read that field. Immediate-heavy CoreMark code
makes `instr[24:20]` a frequent false alias for I-type immediates, while
JAL, LUI, AUIPC, FENCE, and SYSTEM also carry dead source bitfields that
can trigger avoidable one-cycle bubbles. This looks like a real IPC leak,
and the prior attempts failed on contract bugs rather than on benchmarked
fitness.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Pass the full latched IF/ID instruction into `hazard_unit.sv` and derive
compact `uses_rs1` and `uses_rs2` bits locally from opcode plus the small
funct3 cases that matter, then only assert the load-use bubble when the
younger instruction truly consumes the matching register. Keep decode,
RVFI source reporting, redirect precedence, and MEM/DIV stall handling
unchanged. This should recover IPC from false load-use stalls while staying
off the fragile fetch `pc -> next_pc` path and avoiding the added data-path
muxing that regressed in the ready-cycle MEM-to-EX bypass attempt.

title: EX-Classified MEM Stall Path
motivation:
The durable bench wins came from MEM-side decoupling, but the later wide
descriptor and smarter-slot ideas show that extra routing on the memory pipe
quickly loses or fails placement. The remaining structural hotspot is the
`mem_stage -> mem_wait -> hazard_unit` feedback loop, where MEM still
recomputes misalignment trapping and RAM/MMIO classification every cycle
before deciding whether the whole pipeline must hold.

title: Stall-Window JAL Redirect Token Predictor
motivation:
Predictor work on `cores/bench` has repeatedly lost when it adds always-hot
logic to IF or a live decode-to-PC feedback path, but the exact `JAL`
predictor was the closest branch-family miss and the later failures were
about control integration rather than lack of benchmark relevance. The
narrowest remaining predictor opportunity is therefore an exact `JAL` scheme
that only operates inside cycles already lost to older hazards and enters the
pipeline as a real redirect event instead of a raw PC override.

title: RS2-Only Load-Use Stall Filter
motivation:
`hazard_unit.sv` currently treats both IF/ID source fields as live after an
older load, even when the younger opcode is an immediate-form instruction
whose `instr[24:20]` bits are just literal data. The prior full
`uses_rs1`/`uses_rs2` filters were either broken or too timing-expensive,
but the dominant false-positive case is still the raw `rs2` compare against
I-type immediates. A narrower filter aimed only at that field can recover
bubbles without paying for a full decode in the hazard path.

hypothesis.motivation:

but the dominant false-positive case is still the raw `rs2` compare against
I-type immediates. A narrower filter aimed only at that field can recover
bubbles without paying for a full decode in the hazard path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the existing `rs1` load-use check unchanged and only qualify the
`rs2` side of the hazard. Pass a small IF/ID opcode view into
`hazard_unit.sv`, derive a conservative `uses_rs2` bit there, and suppress
the `rd == instr[24:20]` stall only for opcode classes that never read rs2
in this RV32IM core: OP-IMM, LOAD, JALR, LUI, AUIPC, JAL, MISC-MEM, and
SYSTEM. Branches, stores, R-type ops, and unknown/reserved encodings stay
conservative. This directly targets the immediate-heavy false-stall pattern
seen in CoreMark while adding far less hazard-path logic than the earlier
full source-use decoder, so it has a better chance of improving cycles
without giving back the current Fmax win.

Replace the unconditional raw rs2 alias check with a conservative opcode-qualified `uses_rs2` gate, while leaving rs1 handling and existing redirect/mem/div stall precedence unchanged.
Add a focused regression for false load-use stalls on immediate-form consumers whose `instr[24:20]` aliases the older load destination, while keeping true rs2 consumers stalled.

title: Non-Memory EX-to-WB Fast Lane
motivation:
The durable structural wins on `cores/bench` have all come from exploiting
data-side slack without touching the fragile fetch path, but the current
pipeline still forces every ALU, branch, jump, FENCE, and illegal-trap
instruction through the increasingly dense `mem_stage.sv`. Recent structural
retimes that tried to simplify MEM by pushing more metadata into EX lost or
failed placement, which suggests the better direction is to remove the common
non-memory case from that stage instead of widening `EX/MEM`.

hypothesis.motivation:
The durable structural wins on `cores/bench` have all come from exploiting
data-side slack without touching the fragile fetch path, but the current
pipeline still forces every ALU, branch, jump, FENCE, and illegal-trap
instruction through the increasingly dense `mem_stage.sv`. Recent structural
retimes that tried to simplify MEM by pushing more metadata into EX lost or
failed placement, which suggests the better direction is to remove the common
non-memory case from that stage instead of widening `EX/MEM`.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split the back end into two single-retire lanes: keep the existing MEM path
for loads, stores, and the pending-store slot, but add a narrow EX-to-WB fast
lane for one-cycle non-memory instructions whose architectural result is fully
known at EX completion. The fast lane would hold `{pc, instr, rd/result,
rs1/rs2 RVFI data, pc_next, trap}` for ALU, branch, jump, FENCE, and similar
non-memory ops, while memory instructions continue using the current
`ex_mem -> mem_stage -> mem_wb` flow unchanged. A small oldest-first retire
arbiter would preserve strict in-order single-channel RVFI semantics by
draining MEM before the fast lane whenever an older memory op is present, and
would backpressure EX only when the fast lane is occupied behind a still-live
memory op. This targets the now-heavy MEM cone without repeating the failed
"predecode more into EX" pattern, because it shortens the common non-memory
path by bypassing byte-mask/load-align/store-slot logic rather than carrying
more always-hot bits forward.

Emit eligible one-cycle non-memory ops into the new fast lane instead of always sending them through the memory pipeline, while leaving load/store behavior and divide sequencing architecturally intact.

Restrict the dense MEM datapath to true memory traffic and the existing pending-store machinery, so non-memory instructions no longer pay the byte-mask, load-align, and store-slot cone on the common path.
Add any needed backpressure handling for a full fast lane blocked behind an older memory op, while keeping current redirect, load-use, dmem stall, and long-op precedence rules precise.
Wire the fast lane, memory lane, and oldest-first single-retire arbiter together so regfile writeback, RVFI channel 0, and `rvfi_order` remain strictly in order under mixed memory/non-memory traffic.
Add pipeline cases for mixed ALU and memory sequences to prove that non-memory fast-lane bypass preserves in-order retirement, load/store ordering, and RVFI-visible PCs and register writes.

lesson: - 2026-05-15 hyp-20260515-002-r15s1 (regression, -24.09%): On this single-retire core, bypassing non-memory ops around `mem_stage.sv` only pays if they retire directly; mirroring `{pc, rd/result, rs1/rs2, pc_next, ctrl, instr}` into a parallel fast lane and then muxing back into shared `mem_wb_w` duplicates always-hot writeback/RVFI state and arbitration, which costs more fitness than the MEM byte-mask/load-align/store-slot cone it skips.

title: Source-Matched Deferred Exact JAL Predictor
motivation:
Predictor work on this core has only looked plausible when it avoids the
fragile live `pc -> next_pc` decode loop. Conditional-branch, BTB, RAS, and
hold-based schemes all lost on timing or steady bubble cost, but exact JAL
recovery stayed closest to break-even and later failures were dominated by
control-integration bugs rather than clear benchmark irrelevance. JAL is the
narrowest useful target because its redirect is always taken and its target
is exact from `pc + imm`, with no branch compare, history table, or regfile
input on the prediction side.

hypothesis.motivation:
Predictor work on this core has only looked plausible when it avoids the
fragile live `pc -> next_pc` decode loop. Conditional-branch, BTB, RAS, and
hold-based schemes all lost on timing or steady bubble cost, but exact JAL
recovery stayed closest to break-even and later failures were dominated by
control-integration bugs rather than clear benchmark irrelevance. JAL is the
narrowest useful target because its redirect is always taken and its target
is exact from `pc + imm`, with no branch compare, history table, or regfile
input on the prediction side.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry registered exact-redirect token for legal `JAL`
instructions, but make it a first-class, source-matched control event rather
than a raw PC override. When ID sees a valid JAL, latch `{valid, source_pc,
target_pc}` into a tiny token register; on the following cycle IF may consume
it only if the current fetch PC still matches `source_pc` and no older EX
redirect has intervened. Drive a dedicated IF-only squash when the token
fires so the sequential wrong-path fetch is killed while the JAL itself still
advances normally into EX. Carry a compact `predicted_jal` marker alongside
the in-flight JAL so EX preserves canonical `pc_next`, `rd_wdata = pc+4`,
trap, and RVFI behavior, and suppresses only the redundant physical redirect
when the exact token already won. This keeps the common fallthrough path
cheap, avoids live decode-to-PC feedback, and directly addresses the earlier
broken JAL predictors' desynchronization risk.

changes.description: Add compact exact-JAL prediction metadata so the token fire can be tracked cleanly from decode into execute without changing the single-retire RVFI contract.
Detect legal JALs in the decode view and emit the registered `{source_pc, target_pc}` token-arm request plus the sideband marker for the matching in-flight jump.
Consume the one-entry token from flop outputs only, give older EX redirects priority, and issue an IF-only squash when the exact JAL token redirects fetch to its target.
Split IF-only squash from full ID-kill redirect handling so a predicted exact JAL can kill the single wrong-path fetch without bubbling away the JAL currently advancing through decode.
Wire the token register, source-PC match, and arbitration between ID token generation, IF token consumption, hazard control, and the existing EX redirect path.
Keep JAL architecturally authoritative in EX but recognize the exact-token-hit case so EX suppresses only the redundant physical redirect while preserving canonical `pc_next` and writeback semantics.

lesson: - 2026-05-15 hyp-20260515-003-r15s2 (regression, -22.74%): Even a deferred exact `JAL` predictor with a registered `{source_pc,target_pc}` token regressed, because the always-hot IF `pc == source_pc` compare plus `pred_redirect`/`if_squash`/`redirect_raw` arbitration still lengthen fetch/control timing more than CoreMark's sparse unconditional-jump bubbles can repay.
